narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 61% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background that correlate with bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the average of about 5.5; students in lower deciles like yours have historically struggled more on the bar, so this stood out as the top concern for your profile. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 33, lower than the typical 36.7 average, which also nudged the model toward expecting a failure since stronger scores are key predictors of bar passage. Counterbalancing these somewhat was your strong undergraduate GPA, which had the second-strongest influence but in a positive direction, suggesting it helps show your potential to pass despite the other hurdles. Your middle family income bracket and plan to attend full-time—common for over 90% of students—played smaller roles and didn't sway the outcome much. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your profile mirrors many who pass in terms of GPA strength and full-time status, but your weaker Year 3 performance and LSAT pulled you into a higher-risk group for bar failure. While these academic markers were the biggest drivers, it's worth noting that real-world success often hinges on more than predictions like this. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or recommendations, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that weigh your standout GPA more heavily. You could also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bridging these gaps specific to your background. With targeted improvements, a stronger application next cycle could flip this prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—many students in similar spots have turned rejections into successes.
